name: guarddog

metadata.name: guarddog
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License
metadata.summary: GuardDog is a CLI tool to Identify malicious PyPI packages

documentation:
GuardDog
========

See https://github.com/datadog/guarddog

functions.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.utils.get_domain_creation_date
documentation:
Gets the creation date of an domain name

Args:
    domain (str): domain of email address

Returns:
    datetime: creation date of domain
    bool:     if the domain is currently registered

modules.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.npm.direct_url_dependency
documentation:
Direct URL Dependency Detector

Detects if a package depends on direct URL dependencies

classes.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.npm.direct_url_dependency.NPMDirectURLDependencyDetector
documentation:
This heuristic detects packages with direct URL dependencies.
Dependencies fetched this way are not immutable and can be used to inject untrusted code
or reduce the likelihood of a reproducible install.

functions.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.npm.npm_metadata_mismatch.describe_diff
documentation:
Creates a string of the form
Difference between manifest and package.json found:
dependencies:
    key: Manifest("v4.0.0"), package.json("v3.0.1")
scripts:
    key: Manifest("a"), package.json("b")
main:
    Manifest:
        index.js
    package.json
        malicious.js
...

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.npm.typosquatting.NPMTyposquatDetector.detect
documentation:
Uses a package's information from PyPI's JSON API to determine the
package is attempting a typosquatting attack

Args:
    package_info (dict): dictionary representation of PyPI's JSON
        output

Returns:
    list[str]: names of packages that <package_name> could be
    typosquatting from
    @param **kwargs:

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.npm.typosquatting.NPMTyposquatDetector._get_confused_forms
documentation:
Gets confused terms for npm packages.
Currently, there are no confused terms for npm packages.

Detector for typosquatting attacks. Detects if a package name is a typosquat of one of the top 5000 packages.
Checks for distance one Levenshtein, one-off character swaps, permutations
around hyphens, and substrings.

Attributes:
    popular_packages (set): set of top 5000 most popular packages from npm

classes.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.bundled_binary.BundledBinary
documentation: This heuristic detects if the package includes bundles binary file.

classes.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.repository_integrity_mismatch.IntegrityMismatch
documentation: This package contains files that have been tampered with between the source repository and the package CDN

classes.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.go.typosquatting.GoTyposquatDetector

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.go.typosquatting.GoTyposquatDetector.detect
documentation:
Uses a Go package's name to determine the
package is attempting a typosquatting attack

Args:
    name (str): The name of the package,
        also known as the import path

Returns:
    Tuple[bool, Optional[str]]: True if package is typosquatted,
       along with a message indicating the similar package name.
       False if not typosquatted and None

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.go.typosquatting.GoTyposquatDetector._get_confused_forms
documentation:
Gets confused terms for Go packages
Confused terms are:
    - golang to go swaps (or vice versa)
    - the removal of go/golang terms
    - gitlab.com to github.com swaps (or vice versa)

Args:
    package_name (str): name of the package

Returns:
    list: list of confused terms

Detector for typosquatting attacks for go modules. Checks for distance one Levenshtein,
one-off character swaps, permutations around hyphens, and substrings.

Attributes:
    popular_packages (set): set of top 500 most popular Go packages,
      as determined by count of references across top starred repositories

classes.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.empty_information.EmptyInfoDetector

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.empty_information.EmptyInfoDetector.detect
documentation:
Uses a package's information from PyPI's JSON API to determine
if the package has an empty description

Args:
    package_info (dict): dictionary representation of PyPI's JSON
        output

Returns:
    bool: True if package description is empty
    @param **kwargs:

This heuristic detects packages with empty documentation on the package repository.
Such situation might be the marker of a low quality package.

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.pypi.typosquatting.PypiTyposquatDetector._get_confused_forms
documentation:
Gets confused terms for python packages
Confused terms are:
    - py to python swaps (or vice versa)
    - the removal of py/python terms

Args:
    package_name (str): name of the package

Returns:
    list: list of confused terms

Detector for typosquatting attacks. Detects if a package name is a typosquat of one of the top 1000 packages.
Checks for distance one Levenshtein, one-off character swaps, permutations
around hyphens, and substrings.

Attributes:
    popular_packages (list): list of top 5000 downloaded packages from PyPI

modules.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.pypi.repository_integrity_mismatch
documentation:
Empty Information Detector

Detects if a package contains an empty description

functions.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.pypi.repository_integrity_mismatch.find_best_github_candidate
documentation:
This method goes through multiple URLs and checks which one is the most suitable to be used as GitHub URL for
the project repository.
If the repository homepage is a GitHub URL, it is used in priority

functions.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.pypi.repository_integrity_mismatch.dict_generator
documentation:
This generator recursively go through an arbitrary dict
Each iteration will be an array containing the path of all leaves of the dict

functions.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.pypi.repository_integrity_mismatch.exclude_result
documentation:
This method filters out some results that are known false positives:
* if the file is a documentation file (based on its extension)
* if the file is a setup.cfg file with the egg_info claim present on Pypi and not on GitHub

classes.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.pypi.repository_integrity_mismatch.PypiIntegrityMismatchDetector
documentation:
This heuristic compares source code available on the package source code repository (e.g. GitHub), and source code
published on PyPI. If a file is on both sides but has a different content, this heuristic will flag the package.

This helps identify packages whose release artifacts were modified directly on PyPI.

Current gaps:
* Does not check for extraneous files in the release artifacts
* Does not run it parallel, so can be slow for large code bases
Note: we should have the GitHub related logic factored out as we will need it when we check for signed commits

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.typosquatting.TyposquatDetector._is_distance_one_Levenshtein
documentation:
Returns True if two names have a Levenshtein distance of one

Args:
    name1 (str): first name
    name2 (str): second name

Returns:
    bool: True if within distance one

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.typosquatting.TyposquatDetector._is_swapped_typo
documentation:
Returns true is two names are adjacent swaps of each other

Args:
    name1 (str): first name
    name2 (str): second name

Returns:
    bool: True if adjacent swaps

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.typosquatting.TyposquatDetector._generate_permutations
documentation:
Generates all permutations of hyphenated terms of a package

Args:
    package_name (str): name of package

Returns:
    list[str]: permutations of package_name

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.typosquatting.TyposquatDetector._is_length_one_edit_away
documentation:
Returns True if two packages are within a distance one typo edit
(either within a Levenshtein distance of one or an adjacent swap edit)

Args:
    package1 (str): first package name
    package2 (str): second package name

Returns:
    bool: True

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.metadata.typosquatting.TyposquatDetector.get_typosquatted_package
documentation:
Gets all legitimate packages that a given name
is possibly typosquatting from

Checks for Levenshtein distance, permutations, and confused terms
against the top 5000 most downloaded PyPI packages

Args:
    package_name (str): name of package

Returns:
    list[str]: names of packages that <package_name> could be
    typosquatting from

These data class aim to reduce the spreading of the logic

Semgrep rule are language specific
Content of rule in yaml format is accessible through rule_content

methods.name: guarddog.scanners.pypi_package_scanner.PypiPackageScanner.download_package
documentation:
Downloads the PyPI distribution for a given package and version

Args:
    package_name (str): name of the package
    directory (str): directory to download package to
    version (str): version of the package

Raises:
    Exception: "Received status code: " + <not 200> + " from PyPI"
    Exception: "Version " + version + " for package " + package_name + " doesn't exist."
    Exception: "Compressed file for package does not exist."
    Exception: "Error retrieving package: " + <error message>
Returns:
    Path where the package was extracted

classes.name: guarddog.analyzer.analyzer.Analyzer

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.analyzer.Analyzer.analyze
documentation:
Analyzes a package in the given path

Args:
    path (str): path to package
    info (dict, optional): Any package information to analyze metadata. Defaults to None.
    rules (set, optional): Set of rules to analyze. Defaults to all rules.

Raises:
    Exception: "{rule} is not a valid rule."

Returns:
    dict[str]: map from each rule and their corresponding output

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.analyzer.Analyzer.analyze_metadata
documentation:
Analyzes the metadata of a given package

Args:
    path (str): path to package
    info (dict): package information given by PyPI Json API
    rules (set, optional): Set of metadata rules to analyze. Defaults to all rules.

Returns:
    dict[str]: map from each metadata rule and their corresponding output

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.analyzer.Analyzer.analyze_sourcecode
documentation:
Analyzes the source code of a given package

Args:
    path (str): path to directory of package
    rules (set, optional): Set of source code rules to analyze. Defaults to all rules.

Returns:
    dict[str]: map from each source code rule and their corresponding output

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.analyzer.Analyzer.analyze_yara
documentation:
Analyzes the IOCs of a given package

Args:
    path (str): path to package
    rules (set, optional): Set of IOC rules to analyze. Defaults to all rules.

Returns:
    dict[str]: map from each IOC rule and their corresponding output

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.analyzer.Analyzer.analyze_semgrep
documentation:
Analyzes the source code of a given package

Args:
    path (str): path to directory of package
    rules (set, optional): Set of source code rules to analyze. Defaults to all rules.

Returns:
    dict[str]: map from each source code rule and their corresponding output

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.analyzer.Analyzer._format_semgrep_response
documentation:
Formats the response from Semgrep

Args:
    response (dict): response from Semgrep
    rule (str, optional): name of rule to format. Defaults to all rules.
    targetpath (str, optional): root directory of scan. Defaults to None.
        Paths in formatted response will be rooted from targetpath.

Returns:
    dict: formatted response in the form...

    {
        ...
        <rule-name>: [
            {
                <path-to-code:line-num>: <dangerous-code>
                ...
            },
            ...
        ],
        ...
    }

methods.name: guarddog.analyzer.analyzer.Analyzer.trim_code_snippet
documentation: Makes sure the matching code to be displayed isn't too long

Analyzes a local directory for threats found by source code or metadata rules

Attributes:
    ecosystem (str): name of the current ecosystem
    metadata_ruleset (list): list of metadata rule names
    sourcecode_ruleset (list): list of source code rule names
    ioc_ruleset (list): list of ioc rule names

    exclude (list): list of directories to exclude from source code search

    metadata_detectors(list): list of metadata detectors

documentation:
Gives GitHub authentication through access token

guarddog.scanners.scanner.ProjectScanner.scan_remote

classes.name: guarddog.scanners.scanner.PackageScanner

methods.name: guarddog.scanners.scanner.PackageScanner.scan_local
documentation:
Scans local package

Args:
    path (str): Path to the directory containing the package to analyze
    rules (set, optional): Set of rule names to use. Defaults to all rules.
    callback (typing.Callable[[dict], None], optional): Callback to apply to Analyzer output

Raises:
    Exception: Analyzer exception

Returns:
    dict: Analyzer output with rules to results mapping

methods.name: guarddog.scanners.scanner.PackageScanner.scan_remote
documentation:
Scans a remote package

Args:
    * `name` (str): name of the package on PyPI
    * `version` (str, optional): version of package (ex. 0.0.1). If not specified, the latest version is assumed
    * `rules` (set, optional): Set of rule names to use. Defaults to all rules.
    * `base_dir` (str, optional): directory to use to download package to. If not specified, a temporary folder
    is created and cleaned up automatically. If not specified, the provided directory is not removed after the
    scan.
    * `write_package_info` (bool, default False): if set to true, the result of the PyPI metadata API is written
     to a json file

Raises:
    Exception: Analyzer exception

Returns:
    dict: Analyzer output with rules to results mapping

methods.name: guarddog.scanners.scanner.PackageScanner.download_compressed
documentation:
Downloads a compressed file and extracts it

Args:
    url (str): download link
    archive_path (str): path to download compressed file
    target_path (str): path to unzip compressed file

Scans package for attack vectors based on source code and metadata rules

functions.name: guarddog.scanners.go_package_scanner.escape_module_name
documentation: As described in https://go.dev/ref/mod#goproxy-protocol:
> To avoid ambiguity when serving from case-insensitive file systems,
> the $module and $version elements are case-encoded by replacing every uppercase letter
> with an exclamation mark followed by the corresponding lower-case letter.
> This allows modules example.com/M and example.com/m to both be stored on disk,
> since the former is encoded as example.com/!m.

modules.name: guarddog.cli
documentation:
Package Malware Scanner

CLI command that scans a package version for user-specified malware flags.
Includes rules based on package registry metadata and source code analysis.

functions.name: guarddog.cli.cli
documentation:
GuardDog cli tool to detect malware in package ecosystems

Supports PyPI, npm and Go modules.

Example: guarddog pypi scan semantic-version

Use --help for the detail of all commands and subcommands

functions.name: guarddog.cli._get_rule_param
documentation:
This function should return None if no rules are provided
Else a set of rules to be used for scanning

functions.name: guarddog.cli._verify
documentation:
Verify a requirements.txt file

Args:
    path (str): path to requirements.txt file

functions.name: guarddog.cli._scan
documentation:
Scan a package

Args:
    identifier (str): name or path to the package
    version (str): version of the package (ex. 1.0.0), defaults to most recent
    rules (list[str]): specific rules to run, defaults to all

functions.name: guarddog.cli.print_scan_results
documentation: Pretty prints scan results for the console

functions.name: guarddog.cli.exit_with_status_code
documentation: Given the results, exit with the appropriate status code

classes.name: guarddog.cli.CliEcosystem
documentation:
Class that dynamically represents an ecosystem in click
It dynamically selects the ruleset to the instantiated ecosystem
This class is used to create dynamic groups in the cli, each group is an ecosystem with the same options

variables.name: guarddog.utils.config.PARALLELISM
documentation:
This flag specifies if an analysis of all posible versions is required
- True: All possible versions are analyzed
- False [default]: Only best match is analyzed

variables.name: guarddog.utils.config.VERIFY_EXHAUSTIVE_DEPENDENCIES
documentation:
This parameter specifies the location of the top packages cache
- Default: guarddog/analyzer/metadata/resources

variables.name: guarddog.utils.config.TOP_PACKAGES_CACHE_LOCATION
documentation:
This parameter specifies comman separated file extentions that YARA rules will not run against
- Default: ini,md,rst,txt,lock,json,yaml,yml,toml,xml,html,rst,csv,sql,pdf,doc,docx,ppt,
           pptx,xls,xlsx,odt,changelog,readme,makefile,dockerfile,pkg-info
